item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
Honestly I saw this comment in Recent Posts and I thought you were taking about FloppyPhoenix.

Good skip tho, +1
You deserve it, Phoenix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love weird, interesting and imaginative skips like this one, upvoted with with a smile!

item.title: Translations
item.content:encoded:
To promote translations of my work and thank all the authors for their hard work, I am linking to them here in addition to my author page.

  • Chinese by wddzyz
  • Russian by 4opHbIu_6o6p
  • Portuguese by cauazinlindaum

Thank you all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the best SCPs I've read in a while. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is not. The narrative here isn't specific enough to the writing process to really be a critique of that; I more or less included my own experiences dealing with writing in the article.

Thank you for the comment, and I'm glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Write like nobody's watching.
+1

(On another note, is this a subtle critique of cold-posters and the writing process in general?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm So Meta, Even This Acronym

(I enjoyed this. It was an interesting choice to omit most of the research regarding the anomaly itself — SCP articles are always an exercise in omission, but usually people err heavily on the side of including boring-but-relevant detail. The characterization and ambiguous character arc are both really well done — and good dialogue. Nicely done.)
Always get happy seeing one of my darlings reach the +50 milestone. Here's hoping for another 50 and beyond :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Considering the lack of Safe class SCPs lately, this is even more promissing.

Upvoted it because it is immersive and i enjoyed reading it,
but also because it does not take the "2spoopy4me" route.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you have an idea of how they're incomprehensible, I could try my hand. :B I've written some perfectly unreadable shit in my life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay, and upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited slightly to include that in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe:

New text manifests in the current unfinished volume, as though manually typewritten, at an average rate of 604 words per day and a new unfinished volume will manifest when the current volume reaches a conclusion (typically between 10 and 900 pages).

Adjust numbers to suit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Will include, although I guess to nitpick at a couple points.

+ Not to stretch out forums.
- Out of kindness and all!

(as monospaced Courier type)

This would be implied by the fact that Phoenix is writing on a typewriter, isn't it? I did research beforehand and found that that was the case so I didn't think to include that.

at an average rate of 5000 words per day

Pretty ambitious for someone like them, who finds it incredibly hard to write at all :P

and a new unfinished volume will manifest when the current volume reaches between 10 and 900 pages.

Not always the case; volume 51 was only three pages, after all. The original volumes were between those pages at the time of discovery/containment; everything after not necessarily so.

- Out of kindness and all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I legitimately didn't think it'd be important to the entertainment value

I can't speak for anyone else but omissions like this do affect my enjoyment because I find myself rereading and wondering if I missed something or if I don't actually understand what is happening. This is a pretty significant anomalous effect that the Foundation absolutely would note and its omission is very conspicuous in absence.

How could I word it without being unclinical or clunky?

How about:

New text manifests (as monospaced Courier type) in the current unfinished volume at an average rate of 5000 words per day and a new unfinished volume will manifest when the current volume reaches between 10 and 900 pages.

Replacing,"Each volume ranges from 10 to 900 pages."?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The answer for the books is really just "new volumes appear". And for the text, the answer is "when -1 completes a page", both of which were clunk to try and write (my initial draft for this, before I even showed GreenWolf, had the description about 35% longer, almost all of which just trying to explain both mechanisms) before I just cut it entirely. I legitimately didn't think it'd be important to the entertainment value, but both you and a Reddit user commented on it (at least, the book thing). How could I word it without being unclinical or clunky?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is implied here that new text and even new volumes are being produced but as far as I can tell it doesn't actually say this or describe how this is observed, which strikes me as being a pretty critical part of the anomaly. Am I missing this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incredibly relatable and pleasant in itself but it doesn't convey the things that I look for in SCPs the most (mystery, weirdness, thrill, a sinister feeling). A very respectful and appreciative novote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the article maybe short, it sure is sweet.

You do a great job building what I would consider to be two intriguing characters via the dialogue of the interviews. Combining this with the sympathy you had me feeling for Phoenix, and you get yourself a shiny new upvote. Don't spend it all in one place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I've said to TL333s, if I do tales for this, I'll touch on it, but it will be pretty difficult to do so :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP speaks to me. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man hell yes. This reads like an awesome SCP. If you every get inspiration to add more, may I suggest a detailed description of a short volume of one of the books SCP-2246-1 writes (if possibly of course). I'm sure its not much to hear it from someone new, but +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will definitely try it as tale material, although Phoenix… they are not the best or comprehensible writer, which might be hard to pull off well!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just read a fanfic about writing; now I'm reading a skip about writing. Both are good!

If anything, I'd love to see an excerpt of one of these stories, to see, I mean really see, how this guy writes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent job Decibelles!

+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This is a good non-contest skip!

First, the title is a reference to this song by the band Love.

Here, I tried something different for me; more subdued and introspective, as well as characterizing the interviewer. I feel like the interviewers are just as important as the anomalies in SCP articles.

Thank you to the following:

  • GreenWolf for fixing up my clinical tone for me in a big way.
  • ghostchibi for allowing me to use Blake Yamagusuku and looking over their dialog.
  • WrongJohnSilver and Conwell for critiquing this before I posted. WJS gave me one bit of advice that, with minor changes, made the thing more clarified and improved. Never underestimate small changes.

One more thing; both -1 and Yamagusuku use they/them.

+ Ported from my author page discussion thread.
– hide block

I always wanted to write about writer's block; my first idea was a -J that was a block that made it hard to write (hah). I soon threw that away. This was during the period in which I didn't think I wanted to write for the site anymore and my main muse of inspiration (TwistedGears) rapidly drifted away. Up to the point of me writing this article, I had not posted for almost seven months, and hadn't even attempted to write for five straight months.

The idea that this spun off of was "what if the process of writing itself could be an SCP?" and this eventually turned into what it is here. Some people have read into this thinking it was metacommentary on writing; they can choose to take that as their own interpretation, but it wasn't what I intended. I wanted to highlight my very real struggle of how people view my work and how I am perceived on a day-to-day basis, along with the level of scrutiny writing gets. This will sound weird, but I wanted to convey this in a very direct and simplistic way to the majority of readers by making Phoenix very much autistic and give them a lot of the traits that I have. Understanding the world and viewing it through the lens of autism, of course, is not direct or simple, but it's the best way to understand how I feel and how Phoenix feels, and thus convey it easier than being ultra subtle with my showing.

This has been criticized for being too wanky or pretentious. Which is funny, because this skip is everything but wanky and pretentious. The reason why I never show excerpts from Phoenix's writing is because I don't want to dip this article into the field of pretension or lose sight of the main goal, and I wanted it to be as clear and direct as I possibly could.

As for the meaning behind the title, You Set the Scene, well, basically everything from Verse 2 and on.
